params.text:
Health & safety in care

Good health and safety is not paperwork for its own sake — it keeps you, your colleagues and the people you support free from avoidable harm. In adult social care you move between people's own homes, care homes and the community, often working with people who are frail, unsteady on their feet or living with conditions that affect their mobility and awareness. That makes safe working a core part of good care, not an add-on.

The Care Quality Commission (CQC) inspects services against the key question "Is it safe?". Inspectors expect a safe environment, well-maintained equipment, sensible risk assessments and staff who know how to prevent and respond to incidents. When you follow safe practice you are meeting a legal duty and a professional standard at the same time.

  • Protect yourself — back injuries and slips are among the most common reasons care workers are off sick.
  • Protect the people you support — a cluttered hallway or a faulty hoist puts them at risk.
  • Protect your colleagues and visitors — hazards you spot and report help everyone.

params.alt: The five steps of risk assessment

params.question: Health and safety is only the manager's responsibility, so a care worker who spots a frayed hoist sling can leave it for someone else to deal with.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Every worker has a legal duty to take reasonable care and to report hazards. A frayed sling must be removed from use and reported immediately, never left in service.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Everyone shares responsibility. If you spot a hazard like a damaged sling you must take it out of use and report it straight away.
